meta.description: Tier 1 launch wine styles — editorial catalog SSOT for style pages and knowledge graph edges.

faq.a: Yes. It is a dry red with no perceptible sweetness in classic styles.
Cabernet is typically firmer, more tannic, and more structured; Merlot is softer and more approachable.

styles.beginner_notes: If you like bold reds with grip and dark fruit, start here. Pair with fatty protein — the wine needs something to soften against.
styles.seo_title: Cabernet Sauvignon — Wine Style Guide, Profile & Food Pairing
styles.seo_description: What is Cabernet Sauvignon? Full-bodied red wine style with high tannin, dark fruit, and classic steak pairings. Structure, descriptors, and substitutes.
styles.name: Cabernet Sauvignon
styles.summary: Cabernet Sauvignon is a full-bodied red built on high tannin, dark fruit, and oak structure. It is the benchmark steak wine because tannin binds with protein and fat, softening the wine while balancing rich dishes. Classic examples show cassis, cedar, and graphite with firm, age-worthy structure.
styles.serving_notes: Young, tannic examples benefit from 1–2 hours decanting; older wines 30–60 minutes.

faq.a: Generally yes: softer tannin, rounder fruit, and often less angular structure.

styles.beginner_notes: A friendly entry to structured reds — softer than Cabernet but still substantial.
styles.seo_title: Merlot — Wine Style Guide, Profile & Food Pairing
styles.seo_description: Merlot wine style explained: plummy fruit, softer tannin, regions, structure, and food pairings including roast meats.
styles.name: Merlot
styles.summary: Merlot is a medium- to full-bodied red with softer tannin and plummy fruit. It bridges Cabernet's power and Pinot's elegance, pairing with roast meats, mushrooms, and medium-rich dishes. Right Bank Bordeaux and California versions show chocolate and supple texture.
styles.serving_notes: Optional for young oaky examples; 30 minutes is usually enough.

faq.q: Can Pinot Noir pair with fish?
faq.a: Yes — especially salmon and other fatty fish where tannin stays gentle.

avoid_pairings: Very heavy red-meat stews (better with Cabernet)
styles.beginner_notes: Think red fruit, earth, and elegance — one of the most food-friendly light reds.
styles.seo_title: Pinot Noir — Wine Style Guide, Profile & Food Pairing
styles.seo_description: Pinot Noir wine style: light body, red fruit, Burgundy and Oregon classics, and pairings from salmon to roast chicken.
styles.name: Pinot Noir
styles.summary: Pinot Noir is a light- to medium-bodied red with bright acidity, soft tannin, and red fruit. It excels with salmon, roast poultry, and earthy dishes where heavier reds would overpower the plate. Burgundy sets the benchmark for silky cherry and forest-floor complexity.
styles.serving_notes: Usually not required; older Burgundy may need only 20–30 minutes for sediment.

faq.q: Is Syrah the same as Shiraz?
faq.a: Same grape, different naming convention. Shiraz usually signals a riper, New World style.
Rotundone in the grape gives black-pepper aromatics, especially in cooler sites.

styles.beginner_notes: Expect dark fruit and pepper — a bold red that loves the grill. Shiraz tends to be fruitier; Syrah often more savory.
styles.seo_title: Syrah / Shiraz — Wine Style Guide, Profile & Food Pairing
styles.seo_description: Syrah and Shiraz wine style guide: pepper, dark fruit, Rhône vs Australian profiles, structure, and steak and BBQ pairings.
styles.name: Syrah / Shiraz
styles.summary: Syrah (Shiraz in Australia) is a full-bodied red with dark fruit, pepper, and smoky or meaty notes. Northern Rhône Syrah is lean and peppery; Australian Shiraz is riper and more opulent. Both styles handle grilled meats, BBQ, and bold savory dishes.
styles.serving_notes: 1 hour for young, tannic Syrah; 30 minutes for plush Shiraz.

faq.a: Yes. Softer tannin and ripe fruit make it more approachable than many structured reds.

styles.beginner_notes: One of the easiest bold reds to enjoy — soft, fruity, and great with steak night.
styles.seo_title: Malbec — Wine Style Guide, Profile & Food Pairing
styles.seo_description: Malbec wine style: Argentine dark fruit, violet, soft tannin, Mendoza regions, and classic grilled meat pairings.
styles.name: Malbec
styles.summary: Malbec is a plush, full-bodied red with soft tannin, dark fruit, and violet notes. Argentina's Mendoza flagship delivers ripe blackberry and chocolate at approachable prices. It pairs easily with grilled meats and hearty fare without Cabernet's angular grip.
styles.serving_notes: Optional; 30 minutes for young oak-aged bottles.

faq.a: Often yes in Châteauneuf-du-Pape and GSM blends, but single-varietal examples are increasingly common.

styles.beginner_notes: Look for red berry and spice — softer than Cabernet but still substantial with food.
styles.seo_title: Grenache — Wine Style Guide, Profile & Food Pairing
styles.seo_description: Grenache (Garnacha) wine style: red fruit, spice, Rhône and Spanish regions, structure, and Mediterranean food pairings.
styles.name: Grenache
styles.summary: Grenache is a medium- to full-bodied red with red fruit, spice, and often high alcohol but gentle tannin. It shines in Southern Rhône blends and solo in Priorat and Spain's Garnacha heartlands. A versatile match for roast meats, Mediterranean dishes, and charcuterie.
styles.serving_notes: Optional decant for young, high-alcohol examples.

faq.a: Both are Sangiovese. Brunello uses a specific clone, more body, and longer aging requirements.

styles.beginner_notes: High acidity makes Sangiovese a natural at the dinner table — especially Italian food.
styles.seo_title: Sangiovese — Wine Style Guide, Profile & Food Pairing
styles.seo_description: Sangiovese wine style: Chianti and Brunello profiles, cherry and earth, high acidity, and Italian food pairings.
styles.name: Sangiovese
styles.summary: Sangiovese is Italy's signature red grape, built on high acidity, firm tannin, and sour-cherry fruit. Chianti and Brunello di Montalcino define the style — savory, food-driven wines for tomato sauces, grilled meats, and hard cheese. Expect earthy, herbal complexity with age.
styles.serving_notes: 1–2 hours for young Brunello; Chianti often needs less.

faq.a: Thick skins and traditional long maceration build structure meant to age and soften over years.

styles.beginner_notes: Pale color can surprise — focus on aroma and tannin. Give it air and fatty protein.
styles.seo_title: Nebbiolo — Wine Style Guide, Profile & Food Pairing
styles.seo_description: Nebbiolo wine style: Barolo and Barbaresco, tar and rose, high tannin and acidity, decanting tips, and food pairings.
styles.name: Nebbiolo
styles.summary: Nebbiolo is a high-acid, high-tannin Italian red with haunting tar, rose, and cherry aromas despite often pale color. Barolo and Barbaresco are its apex — wines that demand time, decanting, and rich, savory food. Not a casual sipper, but unforgettable with truffle, braised meats, and aged cheese.
styles.serving_notes: Essential for young Barolo — 2–4 hours; older wines about 1 hour.

faq.q: What does Reserva mean on Rioja?
faq.a: Minimum oak and bottle aging requirements — typically more developed, smoother styles.

styles.beginner_notes: Rioja Crianza is an excellent entry — cherry, gentle oak, and easy with food.
styles.seo_title: Tempranillo — Wine Style Guide, Profile & Food Pairing
styles.seo_description: Tempranillo wine style: Rioja and Ribera del Duero, cherry and oak, aging categories, and Spanish food pairings.
styles.name: Tempranillo
styles.summary: Tempranillo is Spain's noble red — medium body, moderate tannin, and red cherry with leather and vanilla from oak. Rioja and Ribera del Duero span youthful joven to long-aged gran reserva styles. A versatile partner for lamb, tapas, and anything off the grill.
styles.serving_notes: 30–60 minutes for reserva and gran reserva.

faq.a: The wine is dry, but ripe fruit can taste jammy. White Zinfandel is a separate, sweet rosé style.

styles.beginner_notes: Expect big, ripe fruit — great for casual cookouts and bold flavors.
styles.seo_title: Zinfandel — Wine Style Guide, Profile & Food Pairing
styles.seo_description: Zinfandel wine style: jammy California red, BBQ and burger pairings, alcohol and structure, and Primitivo connection.
styles.name: Zinfandel
styles.summary: Zinfandel is a bold California classic — high alcohol, jammy black fruit, and spice from ripe grapes and often American oak. It ranges from hearty red table wine to plush old-vine expressions. Built for BBQ, burgers, and anything smoky or sauced.
styles.serving_notes: Optional; 30–60 minutes for young, high-alcohol bottles.

faq.a: Oaked shows vanilla, butter, and weight; unoaked is leaner with brighter fruit and acidity.

avoid_pairings: Very spicy food without dairy balance
Light citrus-only dishes with heavy oak versions
styles.beginner_notes: Try both unoaked and oaked — they can feel like different wines. Oak = cream and toast; unoaked = crisp apple.
styles.seo_title: Chardonnay — Wine Style Guide, Profile & Food Pairing
styles.seo_description: Chardonnay wine style: oaked vs unoaked, buttery vs crisp, Burgundy and California, and creamy dish pairings.
styles.name: Chardonnay
styles.summary: Chardonnay spans lean unoaked styles to rich, buttery oaked examples from barrel fermentation and malolactic conversion. Match weight to the dish: oaked Chardonnay for cream sauces and salmon; unoaked for roast chicken and lighter fare. White Burgundy remains the global benchmark.
styles.serving_notes: Rarely needed; allow 10 minutes in glass for premium white Burgundy.

faq.a: Sancerre is typically leaner and more mineral; Marlborough is fruit-forward with pronounced citrus and herb.

styles.beginner_notes: Think citrus and green notes — one of the most refreshing food whites.
styles.seo_title: Sauvignon Blanc — Wine Style Guide, Profile & Food Pairing
styles.seo_description: Sauvignon Blanc wine style: citrus, grass, Sancerre and Marlborough, high acidity, and goat cheese pairings.
styles.name: Sauvignon Blanc
styles.summary: Sauvignon Blanc is a high-acid, aromatic white with citrus, herb, and mineral notes. Its lift cuts richness and pairs with goat cheese, salads, and lighter proteins. Sancerre is lean and stony; Marlborough is zesty and tropical-leaning.
styles.serving_notes: Serve well chilled — decanting strips freshness.

faq.a: No. Many are dry (Trocken); sweetness varies by region and style.

faq.q: Why pair Riesling with spicy food?
faq.a: Off-dry fruit and acidity soften heat better than high-alcohol dry reds.

styles.beginner_notes: Check the label for sweetness — German terms like Kabinett, Spätlese, and Trocken guide style.
styles.seo_title: Riesling — Wine Style Guide, Profile & Food Pairing
styles.seo_description: Riesling wine style: dry to sweet, Mosel and Alsace, acidity, spice pairing logic, and Thanksgiving matches.
styles.name: Riesling
styles.summary: Riesling ranges from bone-dry to lusciously sweet, always with piercing acidity and aromatic precision. Off-dry styles tame spice; dry styles lift fatty fish and aromatic Asian dishes. German Mosel and Alsace set the quality bar across the sweetness spectrum.
styles.serving_notes: Dry 3–10 years; sweet German examples 10–30+ years.

faq.a: Same grape. Grigio usually means lighter Italian style; Gris often signals richer Alsace character.

styles.beginner_notes: A safe, crowd-pleasing white — light, crisp, and low commitment.
styles.seo_title: Pinot Grigio — Wine Style Guide, Profile & Food Pairing
styles.seo_description: Pinot Grigio wine style: crisp Italian white, light body, seafood and antipasti pairings, and Pinot Gris comparison.
styles.name: Pinot Grigio
styles.summary: Pinot Grigio is a light, crisp white prized for easy drinking and food flexibility. Italian versions are lean and neutral; Alsace Pinot Gris is richer and spicier. Ideal for antipasti, seafood, and warm-weather sipping.

faq.a: A Loire appellation for Chenin Blanc ranging from dry (sec) to sweet (moelleux).

avoid_pairings: Aggressive oak bombs with delicate food
styles.beginner_notes: Check whether the bottle is dry or off-dry — Chenin spans both beautifully.
styles.seo_title: Chenin Blanc — Wine Style Guide, Profile & Food Pairing
styles.seo_description: Chenin Blanc wine style: Loire Vouvray, high acidity, dry to sweet range, and versatile food pairings.
styles.name: Chenin Blanc
styles.summary: Chenin Blanc is a high-acid white capable of dry, off-dry, sparkling, and noble sweet styles. Loire Valley Vouvray and Savennières showcase honeyed fruit, wool, and chalky minerality. One of the most versatile food whites — from salads to spicy fare to creamy dishes.
styles.serving_notes: Optional for aged Savennières; dry 5–15 years, sweet Loire 10–30+ years.

faq.a: Sometimes lightly, but aroma and texture come mainly from the grape itself.

avoid_pairings: Very light, acidic dishes that flatten the wine
styles.beginner_notes: If you like aromatic, plush whites, Viognier is a step up in texture from Sauvignon Blanc.
styles.seo_title: Viognier — Wine Style Guide, Profile & Food Pairing
styles.seo_description: Viognier wine style: apricot, floral, Condrieu, full-bodied white profile, and creamy dish pairings.
styles.name: Viognier
styles.summary: Viognier is an aromatic, full-bodied white with apricot, floral, and sometimes subtle spice. Condrieu in the Northern Rhône is its spiritual home; California and Australia produce lush, perfumed examples. Pairs with rich poultry, cream sauces, and aromatic spices.
styles.serving_notes: Warm slightly if over-chilled.

faq.a: It can be dry or off-dry. Alsace labels indicate sweetness level — look for 'sec' for dry.

styles.beginner_notes: Love-it-or-hate-it aromatics — if you enjoy perfume and spice, start here.
styles.seo_title: Gewürztraminer — Wine Style Guide, Profile & Food Pairing
styles.seo_description: Gewürztraminer wine style: lychee, rose, spice, Alsace aromatics, and spicy Asian food pairings.
styles.name: Gewürztraminer
styles.summary: Gewürztraminer is intensely aromatic — lychee, rose, spice, and ginger leap from the glass. Alsace produces the benchmark dry to off-dry styles. A natural match for spicy Asian cuisine, aromatic cheeses, and dishes with ginger or five-spice.
styles.serving_notes: Drink 1–5 years for primary aromatics.

faq.a: Same grape. Alvarinho in Portugal often appears in Vinho Verde blends.

styles.beginner_notes: Think seaside white — crisp, slightly salty, perfect with fish.
styles.seo_title: Albariño — Wine Style Guide, Profile & Food Pairing
styles.seo_description: Albariño wine style: Rías Baixas, peach and saline notes, crisp acidity, and seafood pairings.
styles.name: Albariño
styles.summary: Albariño is a crisp, saline Atlantic white from Spain's Rías Baixas — peach and citrus with bright acidity and subtle bitterness. It is built for seafood, tapas, and anything from the ocean. Increasingly planted in Portugal (Alvarinho) and the New World.
styles.serving_notes: Serve well chilled.

faq.a: Champagne is sparkling wine from the Champagne region made by traditional method.

avoid_pairings: Very sweet desserts (unless demi-sec or doux)
styles.beginner_notes: Brut is dry — start there. Blanc de Blancs is Chardonnay; Blanc de Noirs uses dark grapes.
styles.seo_title: Champagne — Wine Style Guide, Profile & Food Pairing
styles.seo_description: Champagne wine style: traditional method, brioche and citrus, sweetness levels, and celebratory food pairings.
styles.name: Champagne
styles.summary: Champagne is the benchmark traditional-method sparkling wine — fine bubbles, high acidity, and layers of toast, citrus, and brioche from extended lees aging. Only wines from Champagne, France may use the name. From apéritif to fried foods, its acidity and effervescence cleanse the palate.
styles.serving_notes: Never decant — preserve bubbles. NV 2–5 years; prestige cuvée 10–25+ years.

faq.a: Prosecco uses tank fermentation for fruit-forward freshness; Champagne uses bottle fermentation for more complexity.

avoid_pairings: Rich chocolate dessert (unless extra dry with fruit)
styles.beginner_notes: Brut Prosecco is widely available and easy — think fresh fruit and bubbles.
styles.seo_title: Prosecco — Wine Style Guide, Profile & Food Pairing
styles.seo_description: Prosecco wine style: Italian sparkling, Glera grape, fresh fruit, brunch pairings, and Prosecco vs Champagne.
styles.name: Prosecco
styles.summary: Prosecco is Italy's Charmat-method sparkling wine — fresh, fruity, and approachable with gentle bubbles. Glera grape delivers pear, apple, and white flowers. Ideal for brunch, antipasti, and light fare where Champagne's complexity is not required.
styles.serving_notes: Serve very chilled; drink within 1–2 years of release.

faq.a: Yes — traditional bottle fermentation, though grape varieties and climate differ.

styles.beginner_notes: Great value bubbly — look for Reserva for more toast and depth.
styles.seo_title: Cava — Wine Style Guide, Profile & Food Pairing
styles.seo_description: Cava wine style: Spanish traditional-method sparkling, tapas pairings, and value alternative to Champagne.
styles.name: Cava
styles.summary: Cava is Spain's traditional-method sparkling wine, traditionally from Macabeo, Xarel·lo, and Parellada. It offers Champagne-like structure at friendlier prices — crisp acidity, green apple, and almond notes. Excellent with tapas, fried foods, and salty snacks.
styles.serving_notes: NV 1–3 years; Reserva and Gran Reserva 3–10 years.

faq.a: Most quality rosé today is dry. Sweet rosé exists (e.g. White Zinfandel) but is a separate category.

styles.beginner_notes: Most rosé is dry — color does not indicate sweetness. When in doubt, check the label.
styles.seo_title: Rosé — Wine Style Guide, Profile & Food Pairing
styles.seo_description: Rosé wine style: dry pink wine, Provence to global styles, food flexibility, and serving tips.
styles.name: Rosé
styles.summary: Rosé spans pale Provence-style wines to deeper, fruit-forward examples from short skin contact with red grapes. Generally dry with refreshing acidity, it bridges white and red — perfect for warm weather, picnics, and flexible food pairing from salads to grilled chicken.
styles.serving_notes: Serve chilled; drink within 1–2 years for freshness.

faq.a: Most modern rosé is dry; this style explicitly highlights zero-sweetness, crisp profiles.

avoid_pairings: Sweet barbecue sauce without acid bridge
styles.beginner_notes: If you want guaranteed dry, look for Provence or labels marked 'sec' or 'brut nature' style rosé.
styles.seo_title: Dry Rosé — Wine Style Guide, Profile & Food Pairing
styles.seo_description: Dry rosé wine style: crisp, zero-sweetness pink wine, Provence character, and salmon and salad pairings.
styles.name: Dry Rosé
styles.summary: Dry Rosé emphasizes crisp acidity and minimal residual sugar — the dominant style in Provence, Loire, and premium New World production. Expect red berry, citrus, and mineral notes without sweetness. Built for Mediterranean fare, seafood, and al fresco dining.
styles.serving_notes: Serve well chilled.

faq.q: Why is Provence rosé so pale?
faq.a: Short skin contact and gentle pressing limit color extraction while preserving freshness.

styles.beginner_notes: The pale onion-skin color is intentional — flavor is delicate, not weak.
styles.seo_title: Provence Rosé — Wine Style Guide, Profile & Food Pairing
styles.seo_description: Provence rosé wine style: pale dry pink, Côtes de Provence, Mediterranean pairings, and serving guide.
styles.name: Provence Rosé
styles.summary: Provence Rosé defines the pale, dry, elegant pink wine style — subtle strawberry, white peach, and herb notes with saline minerality. Made primarily from Grenache, Cinsault, and Syrah in southern France. The benchmark for poolside sipping and Mediterranean cuisine.
styles.serving_notes: Serve chilled; best enjoyed young.

faq.a: Ruby is reductive and fruit-forward; Tawny oxidizes in barrel gaining nut, caramel, and dried fruit.

avoid_pairings: Dry savory mains without sweetness bridge
styles.beginner_notes: Start with Late Bottled Vintage (LBV) or 10-Year Tawny — easier than young Vintage Port.
styles.seo_title: Port — Wine Style Guide, Profile & Food Pairing
styles.seo_description: Port wine style: fortified Douro sweet red, Ruby vs Tawny, dessert and cheese pairings, and serving tips.
styles.name: Port
styles.summary: Port is a fortified sweet red (and some white) wine from Portugal's Douro Valley — grape spirit stops fermentation, preserving natural sugar. Ruby Port is fruity and youthful; Tawny shows nut and caramel from extended oxidative aging. Classic with blue cheese, chocolate, and after dinner.
styles.serving_notes: 55–60°F for Ruby; slightly cooler for White Port cocktails. Vintage Port benefits from decanting; Ruby and Tawny typically do not.

faq.a: Fortification plus deliberate heating create stable wines that tolerate oxygen and warmth.

styles.beginner_notes: Try 10-Year Bual or Verdelho — sweet enough for dessert, acidic enough for savory.
styles.seo_title: Madeira — Wine Style Guide, Profile & Food Pairing
styles.seo_description: Madeira wine style: fortified Atlantic wine, caramel and nut flavors, sweetness levels, and food pairings.
styles.name: Madeira
styles.summary: Madeira is a fortified wine from the Portuguese island of Madeira, deliberately heated (estufagem or canteiro) for remarkable longevity and complex caramel, nut, and citrus peel flavors. Ranges from dry Sercial to sweet Malmsey. Virtually immortal once opened — excellent with soup, nuts, and desserts.
styles.serving_notes: 55–65°F depending on sweetness; optional decant for older vintage.

faq.a: No. Fino, Manzanilla, and most Amontillado are dry. PX and cream styles are sweet.

avoid_pairings: Sweet desserts with dry Fino (unless PX)
styles.beginner_notes: Start with Manzanilla — dry, briny, and great with seafood tapas.
styles.seo_title: Sherry — Wine Style Guide, Profile & Food Pairing
styles.seo_description: Sherry wine style: Fino to Oloroso, Jerez fortification, dry vs sweet, and tapas pairings.
styles.name: Sherry
styles.summary: Sherry is a fortified wine from Jerez, Spain — biologically aged Fino and Manzanilla are dry, pale, and saline; Oloroso is oxidative, nutty, and rich. Amontillado bridges both worlds. One of the most food-versatile wines, from almonds and olives to consommé and tapas.
styles.serving_notes: 45–55°F for Fino/Manzanilla; slightly warmer for Oloroso. Serve Fino fresh and chilled.

faq.a: Moscato is aromatic, sweet, and low-alcohol; Prosecco is typically dry and higher in alcohol.

styles.beginner_notes: Low alcohol and gentle sweetness — approachable for newcomers to wine.
styles.seo_title: Moscato — Wine Style Guide, Profile & Food Pairing
styles.seo_description: Moscato wine style: sweet aromatic white, Moscato d'Asti, low alcohol, brunch and dessert pairings.
styles.name: Moscato
styles.summary: Moscato is a lightly sweet, low-alcohol aromatic white — grapey, floral, and often lightly sparkling (frizzante). Moscato d'Asti from Piedmont is the classic: gentle bubbles, peach and orange blossom, and refreshing sweetness. Best with fruit desserts, brunch, and spicy-sweet contrasts.
styles.serving_notes: Serve very chilled; drink young.
